meta.title: How words can harm, and how love repairs
purpose: Help users identify sinful communication patterns clearly and concretely.
Show how words and relational patterns can wound safety, dignity, trust, and clarity.
Keep the focus on conviction, empathy, love, repentance, and repair rather than accusation alone.
Support self-examination and constructive conversations rooted in Scripture.
framing: This section is not meant to weaponize Scripture against another person.
It is meant to help name harmful patterns honestly, understand their impact, and move toward truth, love, repentance, and repair.
The goal is not condemnation, but sober clarity about sin and a practical path toward love.

speech_sins.title: Speech sins
speech_sins.intro: Words matter deeply. Speech can clarify, dignify, and heal—or distort, humiliate, pressure, and wound. These entries focus on sinful patterns expressed through words, tone, framing, and verbal force.

relational_sins.title: Relational sins
relational_sins.intro: Some patterns are not just about words themselves but about how a person handles relationship: presence, repair, ownership, safety, attention, and follow-through.

heart_sins.title: Heart sins
heart_sins.intro: Behind sinful communication are deeper motives and heart patterns. These entries help identify the inward roots that often drive harmful words and relational conduct.

harsh_words.title: Harsh words
harsh_words.definition: Speech that is sharp, forceful, cutting, or verbally punishing in a way that wounds instead of restoring.

scriptures.principle: Rash words pierce like a sword, but the tongue of the wise brings healing.
Speech should build up and give grace rather than corrupt or rot relationship.
Words should be gracious and fitting.

common_forms: sharp tone
biting phrasing
aggressive word choice
verbal force used to overpower
emotionally punishing wording
harm_impact: can make the other person brace or shut down
can make truth harder to receive
can replace safety with fear
can leave the other person feeling small, tense, or verbally overrun

guilt: I spoke in a way that likely caused harm.
The force of my words mattered, not only the correctness of my point.
I need to take responsibility for how I communicated.
shame: I am hopeless, so there is no point changing.
Because I feel exposed, I should defend myself or minimize the impact.
My discomfort matters more than the harm done.
love_led_reframe: Conviction should move me toward honest repair, not self-protective collapse.
I can face the harm in my words without hiding from responsibility.
Love tells the truth without using verbal force as a weapon.

empathy_prompts: How might my words have felt in the other person's body?
Did my tone make honesty feel safer or more dangerous?
Did my speech preserve dignity while addressing the issue?
love_repair: lower verbal intensity and slow down
name the issue without punishing the person
acknowledge the effect of your tone directly
repair dignity explicitly

contempt.title: Contempt / degrading speech
contempt.definition: Speech that treats the other person as beneath respect through mocking, scorning, sneering, degrading, belittling, or reducing them to a defect.

scriptures.principle: Every person bears God's image and must be treated with dignity.
It is inconsistent to bless God and curse people made in His likeness.
Love is patient and kind, not rude or self-exalting.

common_forms: mocking tone
eye-rolling verbal energy
sarcastic belittling
scornful phrasing
talking as though the other person is ridiculous or beneath respect
harm_impact: can strip dignity from the interaction
can make the other person feel despised instead of addressed
can produce shame, fear, or emotional collapse
can destroy trust and safety quickly

guilt: I spoke in a way that likely degraded the other person.
Even if I felt justified, contempt is not love.
I need to repair the attack on dignity, not only the topic.
shame: Because I used contempt, I am beyond repair.
If I feel exposed, I should deny that contempt was present.
If my intention was not hatred, the impact does not matter.
love_led_reframe: Love never requires contempt to make truth clear.
I can reject degrading speech and still address serious wrongdoing.
Real repentance includes restoring dignity where I stripped it.

empathy_prompts: How might contempt have affected the other person's sense of worth or safety?
Did my words feel like correction, or like being looked down on?
Would I feel emotionally safe receiving my own words?
love_repair: name the contempt plainly
acknowledge the degrading impact without excuse
repair dignity directly and specifically
replace scorning language with truthful and respectful language

false_accusation.title: False accusation / distorted framing
false_accusation.definition: Assigning motives, meanings, or wrongdoing without adequate basis, or describing events in a way that bends reality unfairly.

scriptures.principle: God forbids bearing false witness.
Put away falsehood and speak truth with your neighbor.
Speak truth and do not devise evil in your heart.

common_forms: mind-reading motives
global labels instead of observations
turning suspicions into certainties
framing events unfairly
using selective facts to imply something false
harm_impact: can destabilize shared reality
can make the other person feel trapped or misrepresented
can produce confusion, fear, and helplessness
can damage trust in communication itself

guilt: I may have spoken beyond what I actually knew.
I need to distinguish observation from interpretation.
If I distorted reality, I need to correct the record.
shame: If I admit distortion, I lose all credibility forever.
It is safer to double down than to correct myself.
My certainty matters more than fairness.
love_led_reframe: Love is careful with reality.
I can be honest about uncertainty without becoming passive.
Repair includes correcting misrepresentation clearly.

empathy_prompts: How might it feel to be described unfairly?
Did my words make the other person feel known or trapped inside my interpretation?
Did I protect shared reality or bend it?
love_repair: separate facts from interpretation
correct overstatements explicitly
ask questions where certainty is missing
name behavior without falsely assigning motive

lying_or_omission.title: Lying or omission
lying_or_omission.definition: Hiding, curating, withholding, or distorting truth in a way that misleads another person and damages trust.

scriptures.principle: Do not lie to one another.
Concealing wrongdoing blocks mercy; confession opens the door to healing.
What is hidden will eventually be disclosed.

common_forms: partial truth
selective disclosure
leaving out the part that changes the meaning
concealment to avoid consequences
telling the least damaging version only
harm_impact: can shatter trust when discovered
can make the other person question reality and history
can make safety and stability feel impossible
can turn relationship into guesswork

guilt: I did not tell the whole truth.
Concealment is not neutral; it has relational impact.
Repair requires more than being found out—it requires full honesty.
shame: Since I concealed, I might as well keep hiding.
Exposure means there is no path forward.
If I admit the whole truth, I will lose all hope.
love_led_reframe: Love protects trust with truth, not image management.
Conviction should lead to full disclosure, ownership, and repair.
Honesty is part of safety.

empathy_prompts: What happens to another person's nervous system when truth keeps changing?
How might concealment affect their trust in the relationship?
What would it feel like to discover that important truth was withheld from me?
love_repair: tell the whole truth clearly
correct concealment without waiting to be cornered
acknowledge the effect on trust and safety
accept that rebuilding trust may take time and consistency

exaggeration.title: Exaggeration / overstatement
exaggeration.definition: Using overstated or inflated language that makes the situation sound larger, more global, or more final than is truthful.

scriptures.principle: People will give account for careless words.
Speak truth with your neighbor.
Words carry real life-and-death weight.

common_forms: always/never language
inflating one event into a total pattern
overstating intent or impact unfairly
using emotionally loaded absolutes
harm_impact: can make the other person feel misrepresented
can intensify conflict unnecessarily
can reduce clarity and fairness
can make repair harder because reality feels unstable

guilt: I made it sound bigger or more absolute than was fair.
Exaggeration made clarity worse, not better.
I need to restate the issue accurately.
shame: If I admit exaggeration, my concern no longer matters.
It is safer to keep defending the inflated version.
My fear of losing my point matters more than telling the truth well.
love_led_reframe: Love does not need inflation to be heard.
Truth spoken accurately is stronger than emotionally swollen language.
Repair includes making my language smaller and more precise when needed.

empathy_prompts: Did my words make the other person feel fairly described?
Did exaggeration help clarity or just heighten force?
Would I feel safe being addressed in these same exaggerated terms?
love_repair: replace absolute language with concrete specifics
restate the concern with precision
acknowledge where language became inflated
protect fairness even when upset

mockery.title: Mockery
mockery.definition: Using humor, imitation, exaggeration, or derision to belittle another person rather than understand, correct, or love them.

scriptures.principle: Despising a neighbor is sin.
Speech should build up and give grace.
People made in God's likeness must not be verbally degraded.

common_forms: imitating someone to shame them
sarcastic joking that cuts instead of heals
making their weakness into entertainment
using humor to avoid accountability for cruelty
harm_impact: can make the other person feel exposed and unsafe
can create humiliation rather than clarity
can teach them that vulnerability will be punished
can make repair harder because pain is hidden under 'just joking'

guilt: I may have used humor to degrade rather than love.
My joking may have protected me while wounding the other person.
I need to own the humiliating impact, not hide behind playfulness.
shame: If I was mocking, I am beyond change.
Since I was joking, the other person should just move on.
My intention to be funny cancels out the pain caused.
love_led_reframe: Love does not need humiliation to make a point.
I can be playful without turning another person into the target.
Repair means acknowledging that mockery wounds dignity.

empathy_prompts: What might it feel like to be made into a joke during something painful?
Did my humor make honesty feel safe or dangerous?
Would I feel respected if someone joked about me this way?
love_repair: name the mockery directly
acknowledge the humiliation without minimizing it
restore dignity explicitly
stop using humor as cover for contempt

slander.title: Slander
slander.definition: Speaking about another person in a way that damages their name unfairly through false, distorted, or malicious communication.

scriptures.principle: God forbids bearing false witness.
Slander is folly and brings harm.
Slander must be put away along with malice.

common_forms: damaging another person's reputation unfairly
repeating allegations carelessly
presenting distorted impressions as fact
using moral framing to discredit another person
harm_impact: can injure trust and reputation deeply
can isolate the person socially or relationally
can make the person feel defenseless against distorted narratives
can multiply harm beyond the original relationship

guilt: My words may have damaged another person's name unfairly.
I may have spread distortion rather than truth.
Repair may require correcting what I said to others.
shame: If I admit slander, all credibility is gone forever.
It is easier to let the false impression remain.
Because I felt justified, the damage to their name matters less.
love_led_reframe: Love protects truth and refuses to injure a person's name carelessly.
Repentance may require public correction when public harm was done.
Truth and fairness matter even when I am hurt.

empathy_prompts: What would it feel like to know someone is shaping others' view of me unfairly?
How might this affect the other person's trust and safety?
Did I protect truth or amplify injury?
love_repair: correct false impressions clearly
stop repeating distorted versions
repair reputation where possible
refuse to use speech as a weapon beyond the immediate conflict

gossip.title: Gossip
gossip.definition: Sharing another person's private matters, weaknesses, or failures in a way that is unnecessary, disloyal, or not oriented toward help, safety, or righteous accountability.

scriptures.principle: A gossip betrays confidence, but a trustworthy person keeps a matter covered.
A whisperer separates close friends.
Speech should build up and give grace.

common_forms: sharing private details for emotional release
passing along personal weakness without necessity
talking about someone's failure to people who cannot help
using disclosure to gain validation or alliance
harm_impact: can violate privacy and trust
can make the other person feel exposed and unsafe
can damage closeness and relational security
can spread injury beyond the original moment

guilt: I may have shared what was not mine to share.
I may have looked for relief or alliance at the expense of another person's safety.
I need to consider whether my speech was truly necessary and loving.
shame: Since I already shared it, there is no point repairing.
If I felt overwhelmed, disclosure was automatically justified.
The other person's privacy matters less than my need to vent.
love_led_reframe: Love guards what is private unless truth, safety, or rightful accountability requires disclosure.
I can seek support without unnecessarily exposing another person.
Repair may require acknowledging the breach of trust.

empathy_prompts: How would I feel if someone shared this about me without necessity?
Did my words enlarge safety or enlarge exposure?
Was I serving truth and help, or just relieving myself?
love_repair: stop unnecessary disclosure
acknowledge the privacy breach
repair trust directly where possible
be more intentional about who needs to know and why

manipulative_wording.title: Manipulative wording
manipulative_wording.definition: Using language to pressure, corner, guilt, confuse, or control another person rather than inviting honest and free response.

scriptures.principle: Love does not insist on its own way.
We do not domineer over others, but work with them for joy.
Love seeks the other's good rather than self-advantage.

common_forms: guilt-laden phrasing
cornering questions
stacking the language so only one answer feels allowed
emotional pressure disguised as honesty
harm_impact: can make the other person feel trapped instead of free
can replace consent with pressure
can create confusion about what is actually being asked
can train the relationship toward fear rather than trust

guilt: My wording may have pressured rather than invited.
I may have used language to steer the outcome instead of honoring freedom.
I need to own where my words tried to control the other person's response.
shame: If I was manipulative, I am irredeemable.
Because I wanted a good outcome, the pressure does not matter.
If the other person finally gave in, the method was acceptable.
love_led_reframe: Love makes room for freedom, clarity, and consent.
I can ask honestly without forcing the answer.
Repair includes removing pressure and naming it honestly.

empathy_prompts: Did my words leave real room for a different answer?
How might it feel to be pressured by language instead of invited honestly?
Did I seek clarity, or control?
love_repair: remove loaded wording
ask clearly and simply
accept no without punishment or pressure
repair the sense of being cornered

sarcasm.title: Sarcasm
sarcasm.definition: Using cutting, indirect, or ironic language to wound, expose, belittle, or avoid straightforward truth-telling.

scriptures.principle: Speech should build up and give grace rather than rot relationship.
It is destructive to wound someone and then dismiss it as joking.
Words should be gracious and fitting.

common_forms: saying something cutting under the cover of humor
using irony to express contempt indirectly
making painful points through mocking tone
avoiding direct truth by using sharp wit
harm_impact: can make the other person feel mocked rather than addressed
can create confusion because intent is disguised
can make honesty feel unsafe
can hide hostility inside plausible deniability

guilt: My sarcasm may have carried contempt or hostility.
Indirect sharpness may have caused more harm than direct honesty would have.
I need to own the wound, not hide behind cleverness.
shame: If I was sarcastic, I am only cruel and beyond repair.
Because I did not say it literally, the pain does not count.
If I can explain the joke, I do not need to acknowledge the harm.
love_led_reframe: Love does not need indirect cruelty to communicate truth.
I can be direct, honest, and gentle instead of sharp and disguised.
Repair includes naming the hidden hostility in sarcastic speech.

empathy_prompts: What might it feel like to receive pain hidden inside humor?
Did my sarcasm clarify, or did it make the other person feel ridiculed?
Would I feel emotionally safe if someone spoke to me this way?
love_repair: name the sarcasm directly
acknowledge the cutting effect without minimizing it
replace indirect hostility with clear truth
choose honesty without verbal edge

verbal_intimidation.title: Verbal intimidation
verbal_intimidation.definition: Using tone, volume, force, or words in a way that pressures, overwhelms, or frightens another person into submission rather than inviting truthful and safe engagement.

scriptures.principle: The Lord's servant must not be quarrelsome, but kind and gentle.
A gentle answer turns away wrath.
Love is patient and kind, not rude or forceful.

common_forms: loudness used to overpower
threatening verbal energy
rapid-fire force meant to overwhelm
speaking in a way that makes disagreement feel dangerous
harm_impact: can make the other person freeze, collapse, or comply out of fear
can replace truth with survival responses
can make communication feel physically or emotionally unsafe
can train the relationship toward appeasement instead of honesty

guilt: My speech may have felt overpowering or frightening.
The force of my communication may have silenced truth rather than serving it.
I need to own the fear or pressure my words created.
shame: If my tone was intimidating, I am permanently dangerous with no path to change.
Because I felt intensely, force was unavoidable.
If I was making a true point, the intimidation does not matter.
love_led_reframe: Love does not require fear to be effective.
I can speak strongly without making the other person feel unsafe.
Repair includes reducing force and restoring safety.

empathy_prompts: How might my tone or force have felt in the other person's body?
Did my communication invite honesty, or did it push them into survival mode?
Would I feel emotionally safe with someone speaking to me this way?
love_repair: lower volume and intensity
acknowledge the intimidating effect directly
slow down and create room for response
restore a sense of safety before pressing the point further

weaponized_truth.title: Weaponized truth
weaponized_truth.definition: Using true information in a way that is harsh, humiliating, timed to wound, or aimed at domination rather than restoration.

scriptures.principle: Truth must be spoken in love.
Wisdom from above is pure, peaceable, gentle, and open to reason.
Love rejoices with the truth, not with harm.

common_forms: using truth to win rather than heal
bringing up true things at the most wounding moment
using true details to humiliate
treating accuracy as permission for cruelty
harm_impact: can make the other person fear truth instead of trust it
can mix honesty with humiliation
can produce shame rather than repentance
can make correction feel like attack

guilt: Even if what I said was true, I may have used truth in an unloving way.
I may have cared more about force or exposure than restoration.
I need to own not just whether I was right, but how I handled truth.
shame: If I used truth badly, I should avoid hard truths altogether.
As long as it was accurate, love is secondary.
If the other person was wrong, the wounding delivery does not matter.
love_led_reframe: Truth and love must stay together.
Love aims for restoration, not domination through accuracy.
I can tell the truth in a way that preserves dignity and invites repentance.

empathy_prompts: How might it feel to have true things used in a wounding way?
Did my truth-telling invite growth, or did it mainly expose and injure?
Would I want truth brought to me in this same manner?
love_repair: acknowledge the unloving use of truth
repair the humiliating effect directly
bring truth with gentleness and timing that serves restoration
separate honesty from verbal force

minimization.title: Minimization
minimization.definition: Using language that reduces, shrinks, softens, or dismisses the seriousness of another person's pain, concern, or the impact of harmful behavior.

scriptures.principle: Love enters another person's grief rather than reducing it.
Attend to the interests and experience of the other person.
Responding before truly understanding is folly.

common_forms: it wasn't that bad
you're overreacting
that shouldn't have hurt you so much
focusing on intent to shrink impact
calling real harm small, minor, or insignificant
harm_impact: can make the other person feel unseen or foolish for hurting
can intensify pain by removing validation
can block repair by shrinking the actual problem
can train the relationship toward self-doubt and silence

guilt: I may have reduced something that felt large and real to the other person.
My need for comfort may have led me to make the harm sound smaller than it was.
Repair requires naming impact honestly, not shrinking it.
shame: If I minimized, I must be incapable of empathy forever.
Because I did not mean to dismiss them, the minimizing does not matter.
My discomfort with their pain is more important than understanding it.
love_led_reframe: Love does not need to reduce pain in order to stay present with it.
I can let the other person's experience be real without collapsing.
Repair includes expanding my understanding rather than shrinking their pain.

empathy_prompts: What might it feel like to have real pain treated as small?
Did my words help the other person feel understood or doubted?
Would I feel safe sharing pain with someone who responded this way?
love_repair: acknowledge that you minimized the pain or impact
restate the harm more honestly and specifically
let the other person describe the weight of it without interruption
repair the invalidating effect directly

dismissiveness.title: Dismissiveness
dismissiveness.definition: Responding in a way that brushes off, waves away, or treats another person's concern as unworthy of serious attention.

scriptures.principle: Humility treats others and their concerns as significant.
Be quick to hear and slow to speak.
Love honors the other person.

common_forms: that's not important
you're making too much of this
quickly brushing away concerns
responding with annoyance instead of curiosity
treating the issue as beneath serious attention
harm_impact: can make the other person feel unimportant
can discourage future honesty
can communicate disrespect or emotional carelessness
can deepen isolation in conflict

guilt: I may have treated the other person's concern as less important than it was.
My response may have communicated annoyance rather than care.
Love pays attention instead of brushing people off.
shame: If I was dismissive, I should avoid hard conversations altogether.
Because I thought the issue was small, my tone does not matter.
The other person's concern matters less than my irritation.
love_led_reframe: Love makes room for what matters to the other person.
I can stay curious even when I do not yet understand the weight of the concern.
Repair includes treating their concern with more seriousness and honor.

empathy_prompts: What might it feel like to bring up something painful and be brushed off?
Did my words communicate that the other person mattered?
Would I feel respected if my concern were answered this way?
love_repair: acknowledge the dismissive response plainly
invite the other person to restate the concern fully
respond with curiosity instead of brushing it aside
repair the disrespect communicated by the dismissal

emotional_invalidation.title: Emotional invalidation
emotional_invalidation.definition: Telling, implying, or communicating that another person's emotional experience is wrong, excessive, irrational, or not meaningful enough to take seriously.

scriptures.principle: Enter another person's sorrow instead of denying it.
Bear one another's burdens.
Understanding must come before response.

common_forms: you shouldn't feel that way
that feeling doesn't make sense
telling someone their emotional response is wrong
arguing against their inner experience instead of trying to understand it
harm_impact: can make the other person feel crazy or alone
can block emotional safety and honesty
can create self-doubt and confusion
can turn vulnerable disclosure into danger

guilt: I may have argued against the other person's emotional reality instead of understanding it.
My response may have made their inner world feel unsafe to share.
Love listens to emotional experience before trying to interpret it.
shame: If I invalidated someone, I should stop speaking at all.
Because I disagreed with their interpretation, their emotion did not need care.
Correcting their feelings was more important than understanding them.
love_led_reframe: Love does not require full agreement before offering understanding.
I can acknowledge another person's emotional experience without surrendering truth.
Repair includes making their internal reality safer to express.

empathy_prompts: What would it feel like to be told my feelings are wrong?
Did my response invite deeper honesty or shut it down?
Would I feel emotionally safe if my own distress were handled this way?
love_repair: acknowledge the invalidating effect clearly
reflect back the emotion before analyzing it
make room for their experience without immediate correction
repair the loneliness or confusion created

shaming_speech.title: Shaming speech
shaming_speech.definition: Using words that target identity, worth, dignity, or personhood in a way that produces humiliation, exposure, or a sense of being fundamentally bad rather than called to repentance.

scriptures.principle: Every person bears God's image and must be treated with dignity.
Speech should build up and give grace.
Correction should be gentle and aimed toward repentance.

common_forms: attacking character instead of addressing behavior
using labels meant to degrade identity
exposing weakness in a humiliating way
speaking as though the person's worth is the real issue
harm_impact: can produce collapse, hiding, or despair rather than repentance
can strip dignity and safety from the conversation
can make correction feel unbearable
can wound identity rather than clarify wrongdoing

guilt: My words may have targeted identity rather than behavior.
I may have produced humiliation where truthful correction was needed.
Love calls sin out without making personhood itself the target.
shame: If I used shaming speech, I am beyond change.
If the behavior was serious, humiliation was justified.
The other person's collapse is just proof they should feel bad.
love_led_reframe: Love corrects without degrading.
Conviction points to wrongdoing; shame attacks the self.
Repair includes restoring dignity where my words damaged it.

empathy_prompts: What might it feel like to have my identity attacked instead of my behavior addressed?
Did my words call for repentance or push toward collapse and hiding?
Would I feel safe being corrected this way?
love_repair: name the degrading or identity-targeting speech directly
distinguish behavior from personhood clearly
repair the humiliation and loss of dignity
restate the concern in a way that preserves worth

stone_cold_correction.title: Stone-cold correction
stone_cold_correction.definition: Delivering correction with factual accuracy but emotional coldness, lack of tenderness, or detached force that leaves truth feeling hard, unsafe, or punishing.

scriptures.principle: Truth must be spoken in love.
Wisdom from above is gentle and open to reason.
Correction should be kind and gentle.

common_forms: correcting accurately but without tenderness
using emotionally cold tone in serious moments
speaking truth with detachment that feels punishing
valuing correctness over care
harm_impact: can make truth feel hostile instead of healing
can cause the other person to brace even when the content is valid
can create emotional distance in the middle of correction
can turn needed truth into an experience of coldness

guilt: I may have spoken accurately but without love.
My delivery may have made truth harder to receive.
Correction without tenderness can still do harm.
shame: If I was cold, I should never correct anything again.
Because the content was true, the tone does not matter.
Care is optional as long as I am factually right.
love_led_reframe: Truth and tenderness belong together.
Love does not remove correction; it changes how correction is delivered.
Repair includes acknowledging the coldness that accompanied the truth.

empathy_prompts: How might cold correction feel in the other person's body?
Did my truth arrive as care, or as emotional ice?
Would I feel safe being corrected with this same posture?
love_repair: acknowledge the cold or punishing delivery
restate truth with warmth and humanity
show that the person's dignity matters while correcting
repair the distance created by emotional coldness

accusatory_questioning.title: Accusatory questioning
accusatory_questioning.definition: Using questions not to understand, but to corner, expose, imply guilt, trap, or pressure the other person toward a predetermined conclusion.

scriptures.principle: Understanding should come before response.
Humility seeks the good of the other person rather than leverage.
Love does not insist on its own way.

common_forms: questions designed to corner rather than clarify
stacking questions in a prosecuting way
asking with implied guilt already built in
using questions to force confession or emotional collapse
treating inquiry as a tool of pressure
harm_impact: can make the other person feel trapped rather than understood
can replace safety with interrogation
can produce fear, confusion, or compliance instead of honesty
can make conversation feel like prosecution instead of relationship

guilt: I may have used questions as a weapon rather than a path toward understanding.
My questioning may have pressured instead of invited honesty.
Love asks in order to understand, not to trap.
shame: If I questioned this way, I should never ask hard questions again.
Because I wanted truth, the pressure in my questioning does not matter.
If the other person felt cornered, that only proves they had something to hide.
love_led_reframe: Love uses questions to clarify, not to dominate.
I can ask difficult things while still preserving safety and dignity.
Repair includes acknowledging when my questions became coercive.

empathy_prompts: What might it feel like to be questioned in a way that already assumes guilt?
Did my questions create room for honesty, or did they create fear?
Would I feel safe answering questions delivered this way?
love_repair: acknowledge the interrogating or cornering effect directly
slow down and ask one clear question at a time
separate inquiry from accusation
restore safety before pursuing more detail

misuse_of_confession.title: Misuse of confession
misuse_of_confession.definition: Using confession language in a partial, strategic, vague, image-protective, or self-relieving way that avoids the full truth, true ownership, or meaningful repair.

scriptures.principle: Confession should be honest and real.
Concealing wrong blocks mercy; true confession opens the way.
Repentance names sin clearly rather than hiding inside vagueness.

common_forms: confessing only the least damaging part
vague apology without naming what was done
using confession to relieve inner pressure without repairing impact
performing repentance language while staying guarded
saying just enough to sound accountable
harm_impact: can make the harmed person feel manipulated instead of cared for
can deepen mistrust because confession does not feel solid
can replace real repair with spiritual or emotional performance
can leave the true issue still hidden

guilt: I may have used confession more to manage myself than to tell the truth fully.
My words may have sounded repentant while still withholding reality.
Love confesses clearly, concretely, and honestly.
shame: If my confession was partial, there is no point telling the fuller truth now.
Because I admitted something, the rest does not matter.
If I say spiritual words, the repair is already complete.
love_led_reframe: Confession is not performance; it is truth in the light.
Love does not use repentance language to avoid real ownership.
Repair may require fuller truth, clearer naming, and follow-through.

empathy_prompts: What does it feel like to hear words of confession that still do not feel fully real?
Did my confession make trust easier or harder?
Would I feel safe resting in the kind of confession I offered?
love_repair: confess specifically and concretely
say the fuller truth instead of the safest version
name the impact that still needs repair
let confession lead into action, not just relief

stonewalling.title: Stonewalling / withholding presence
stonewalling.definition: Shutting down, disappearing, going silent, or withdrawing without enough orientation or care, leaving the other person in uncertainty and distress.

scriptures.principle: Look not only to your own interests, but also to the interests of others.
Do not let anger remain unresolved in a way that gives room for further harm.
As far as it depends on you, pursue peace.

common_forms: silence without explanation
sudden disengagement
emotionally leaving while the issue remains open
punitive withdrawal
withholding responsiveness
harm_impact: can leave the other person braced, confused, or abandoned
can keep their nervous system stuck in uncertainty
can turn regulation needs into relational fear
can make repair feel inaccessible

guilt: My withdrawal may have intensified distress rather than containing it.
I may have left the other person carrying uncertainty alone.
I need to distinguish needed pause from withholding.
shame: Because I withdrew badly, I should avoid the issue altogether.
My discomfort justifies indefinite silence.
If I feel flooded, orientation no longer matters.
love_led_reframe: Love can pause without abandoning.
Love provides clarity, timing, and return.
Repair includes closing loops created by silence.

empathy_prompts: What might my silence have felt like to the other person?
Did my withdrawal communicate regulation, or punishment, or disappearance?
Would I feel safe if someone left me this way?
love_repair: state clearly that you need a pause
give a concrete return time
signal care while stepping away
return and close the loop when you said you would

blame_shifting.title: Blame-shifting
blame_shifting.definition: Avoiding ownership by relocating responsibility onto the harmed person, the circumstances, stress, or something else.

scriptures.principle: Blame-shifting is an old human reflex, not repentance.
Repentance says, 'I know my transgressions.'
Confess sins honestly.

common_forms: yes, but you...
I only did that because...
making the other person's pain the real problem
using context as excuse instead of explanation
moving quickly from ownership to counter-accusation
harm_impact: can make the harmed person feel unseen and burdened
can block actual repair
can create confusion about responsibility
can deepen mistrust and emotional exhaustion

guilt: I shifted focus away from what I did.
I tried to reduce my discomfort by relocating responsibility.
Repair requires ownership before explanation.
shame: If I own this cleanly, I will be crushed.
Self-protection is more urgent than responsibility.
As long as context exists, ownership can stay partial.
love_led_reframe: Love tells the truth about self before reacting to the other's contribution.
Ownership is not humiliation; it is part of repair.
I can name context without using it to erase responsibility.

empathy_prompts: How might blame-shifting land on someone already hurt by my behavior?
Did my response make repair easier or harder to trust?
What would it feel like to have someone move away from ownership while I was in pain?
love_repair: name what you did first
separate causes from excuses
do not counter-accuse in the same breath as confession
ask what impact needs to be acknowledged directly

defensiveness.title: Defensiveness
defensiveness.definition: Protecting self-image so quickly that listening, empathy, and ownership are pushed aside.

scriptures.principle: Be quick to hear and slow to speak.
Answering before listening is folly and shame.
Humility looks outward rather than guarding self-importance.

common_forms: instant self-explanation
arguing before understanding
protecting intent over acknowledging impact
treating correction as attack
turning toward self instead of the other's pain
harm_impact: can make the other person feel impossible to reach
can force them to work harder to be understood
can stall repair
can make pain feel minimized or unsafe to express

guilt: I prioritized self-protection over understanding.
My defensiveness likely made the other person's pain harder to express.
Repair requires listening before defending.
shame: If I am corrected, I am being condemned as a person.
My first task is to prove I am not the bad one.
If I feel exposed, I am justified in arguing immediately.
love_led_reframe: Love makes room for another person's pain before self-explanation.
Listening is not surrendering all reality; it is honoring relationship.
I can slow down and reflect impact first.

empathy_prompts: What did my defensiveness require the other person to carry?
Did my response invite honesty or shut it down?
How hard did I make it for the other person to feel heard?
love_repair: reflect impact before explaining intent
ask what felt most painful
pause argument mode
make understanding the first task

emotional_neglect.title: Emotional neglect
emotional_neglect.definition: Repeatedly failing to notice, respond to, or care about another person's emotional reality in a way that leaves them relationally alone.

scriptures.principle: Weep with those who weep.
Bear one another's burdens.
Love must be in deed and truth, not word only.

common_forms: not following up when distress is visible
staying detached while the other person is overwhelmed
ignoring emotional bids for care
remaining task-focused while relationship is hurting
harm_impact: can make the other person feel alone and unimportant
can deepen pain by leaving it unshared
can reduce trust that care will come when needed
can create chronic relational loneliness

guilt: I may have left the other person alone in their pain.
My lack of attunement may have had real impact.
Love requires attention, not only absence of overt harm.
shame: Because I missed it, I am only a bad person.
If attunement does not come naturally, it is not my responsibility.
Since I did not intend harm, the loneliness caused does not matter.
love_led_reframe: Love notices and responds.
I can grow in attentiveness and emotional presence.
Repair includes acknowledging the loneliness my absence created.

empathy_prompts: What might it feel like to hurt next to me and still feel alone?
Did I communicate care through attention and follow-through?
Would I feel emotionally held if I were in their place?
love_repair: acknowledge the loneliness or pain left unaddressed
ask follow-up questions and stay present
practice noticing distress sooner
treat emotional care as part of faithfulness

coercion.title: Coercion / control
coercion.definition: Using pressure, consequences, leverage, fear, guilt, or power imbalance to force another person's response instead of honoring their agency and consent.

scriptures.principle: Kingdom leadership rejects domineering control.
Do not domineer, but be an example.
Love does not insist on its own way.

common_forms: using fear to secure agreement
guilting someone past their limits
making refusal feel unsafe
using relationship pressure to force compliance
harm_impact: can replace freedom with fear
can make the other person feel trapped or overpowered
can damage consent and safety
can teach the relationship that honesty is dangerous

guilt: I may have prioritized my desired outcome over the other person's freedom.
My pressure may have made consent less real.
Love cannot be built on force.
shame: Because I wanted something strongly, pressure was unavoidable.
If I let go of control, everything will collapse.
If the other person complied, the process was acceptable.
love_led_reframe: Love honors agency and consent.
I can ask, invite, and influence without coercing.
Repair includes acknowledging where the other person felt trapped.

empathy_prompts: Did the other person have real room to say no?
What might it feel like to be pressured into agreement?
Did I protect freedom, or control it?
love_repair: remove leverage and pressure
acknowledge the trapped feeling you created
honor no and pace immediately
rebuild trust through non-coercive consistency

boundary_violation.title: Boundary violation
boundary_violation.definition: Ignoring, minimizing, pressuring past, or repeatedly testing another person's stated limits, pace, consent, or capacity.

scriptures.principle: Love does no wrong to a neighbor.
Attend to the interests of others.
Treat others as you would want to be treated.

common_forms: pushing after a clear no
arguing with limits instead of honoring them
minimizing another person's capacity or pace
repeatedly pressing beyond stated boundaries
harm_impact: can make the other person feel unsafe with their own limits
can undermine trust and consent
can produce bracing, shutdown, or fear
can teach that honesty about limits will be punished

guilt: I may have treated another person's limit as negotiable when it was not.
I may have made safety harder by pushing past their boundary.
Love honors limits quickly and consistently.
shame: If I crossed a boundary, all hope is lost.
If I did not mean harm, the boundary does not really count.
Their limit matters less than my urgency.
love_led_reframe: Love protects another person's no.
I can slow down and honor pace without losing love or truth.
Repair includes proving that boundaries are safe with me.

empathy_prompts: What might it feel like if my limit was treated as something to push through?
Did I make boundaries safer or less safe to express?
Would I trust someone who kept testing my no?
love_repair: stop immediately when a limit is named
acknowledge the crossing without argument
repair the sense of unsafety directly
show consistency in honoring future boundaries

weaponized_silence.title: Weaponized silence
weaponized_silence.definition: Using silence, withdrawal, or unresponsiveness as leverage, punishment, control, or emotional pressure rather than as a clearly communicated need for pause or regulation.

scriptures.principle: Look to the interests of others, not only your own.
Do not leave anger unresolved in a way that deepens harm.
As far as it depends on you, pursue peace.

common_forms: ignoring messages to punish
withholding response to increase anxiety
using silence to force compliance or apology
making the other person sit in uncertainty without orientation
harm_impact: can create fear, confusion, and emotional bracing
can make the other person feel punished and powerless
can trap them in uncertainty and self-doubt
can damage trust in pauses and space themselves

guilt: My silence may have functioned as punishment rather than regulation.
I may have used distance to control the other person's internal state.
I need to own the anxiety or fear my silence created.
shame: If I used silence badly, I should avoid all space or pause forever.
Because I needed distance, the impact of my silence does not matter.
If I do not speak, I cannot be held responsible for the effect.
love_led_reframe: Love can pause without punishing.
Love communicates orientation, timing, and care when distance is needed.
Repair includes making silence safer and more understandable.

empathy_prompts: What might my silence have done to the other person's nervous system?
Did my silence communicate regulation or punishment?
Would I feel safe if someone used silence on me this way?
love_repair: acknowledge the punitive or controlling use of silence
differentiate pause from punishment clearly
provide return times and orientation in the future
repair the uncertainty and fear caused

withholding_repair.title: Withholding repair
withholding_repair.definition: Refusing, delaying, minimizing, or evading the steps needed to acknowledge harm, restore trust, and move toward peace.

scriptures.principle: Move toward reconciliation when relationship is ruptured.
Aim for restoration and live in peace.
Pursue peace where possible.

common_forms: refusing to revisit unresolved harm
giving partial repair that avoids the real issue
treating apology as optional or excessive
delaying repair until the other person gives up
harm_impact: can leave pain lingering and uncontained
can make the harmed person feel they must carry the full burden
can erode trust in future repentance
can turn unresolved harm into chronic distance

guilt: I may be resisting the work real repair requires.
My avoidance may be prolonging the other person's pain.
Love does not leave repair half-done when repair is needed.
shame: If repair feels hard, it is better not to start at all.
Because I feel ashamed, I am excused from completing repair.
If the other person is still hurt, there is no point trying.
love_led_reframe: Love moves toward completion, not avoidance.
Repair is costly, but unfinished repair costs relationship too.
I can take one concrete step toward restoring what was harmed.

empathy_prompts: What is it like to wait for repair that never fully comes?
Did my avoidance leave the other person carrying unresolved pain alone?
What would it feel like if my own hurt were treated this way?
love_repair: acknowledge what remains unrepaired
return to the issue instead of drifting away from it
ask what needs to be addressed specifically
follow through until repair is more complete and clear

using_spiritual_language_to_deflect.title: Using spiritual language to deflect
using_spiritual_language_to_deflect.definition: Invoking God, Scripture, prayer, forgiveness, submission, or spiritual ideals in a way that avoids ownership, silences pain, pressures the other person, or redirects attention away from the actual harm.

scriptures.principle: Truth must be spoken in love.
Wisdom from above is gentle, peaceable, and open to reason.
Do not load burdens onto others while avoiding true care.

common_forms: quoting Scripture to avoid accountability
using forgiveness language to bypass repair
spiritualizing the conflict instead of owning behavior
using religious language to pressure the other person into silence
harm_impact: can make truth and Scripture feel unsafe
can silence the harmed person under moral pressure
can deepen confusion and spiritual injury
can block real repentance by replacing it with religious framing

guilt: I may have used spiritual language to protect myself instead of serving truth.
I may have turned something sacred into a shield against ownership.
I need to apply truth to myself before I use it to confront another person.
shame: If I used spiritual language badly, I should avoid spiritual truth entirely.
Because I mentioned good things, the misuse does not matter.
If my words sounded biblical, they must have been loving.
love_led_reframe: Truth must serve repentance and healing, not control or deflection.
Love applies Scripture with humility, gentleness, and integrity.
Repair includes acknowledging spiritual pressure or injury directly.

empathy_prompts: How might it feel to have Scripture or spiritual language used against pain instead of for healing?
Did my words bring light, or did they create pressure and confusion?
Would I feel spiritually safe receiving these words in this way?
love_repair: acknowledge the deflecting use of spiritual language
stop using theology to avoid concrete ownership
apply Scripture to your own repentance first
repair any spiritual confusion or pressure caused

invalidating_experience.title: Invalidating another person's experience
invalidating_experience.definition: Refusing, overriding, or flattening another person's account of what happened or how it affected them in a way that treats their lived experience as untrustworthy or irrelevant.

scriptures.principle: Understanding should come before answering.
Attend to the interests and experience of the other person.
Draw near to another person's sorrow rather than dismissing it.

common_forms: telling the other person that what they experienced is not true
flattening their account into your preferred version
treating their perspective as inherently unreliable
overriding their lived experience with your interpretation
harm_impact: can produce confusion and self-doubt
can make the other person feel erased
can destabilize shared reality
can make communication feel unsafe and futile

guilt: I may have treated the other person's experience as less real than my interpretation.
My response may have made them feel erased or crazy.
Love takes lived experience seriously even when fuller understanding is still needed.
shame: If I invalidated their experience, nothing I say can ever help again.
Because I experienced it differently, their account did not deserve real attention.
If I feel certain, their inner reality matters less.
love_led_reframe: Love makes room for another person's experience before trying to master it.
I can seek fuller truth without erasing what they lived through.
Repair includes acknowledging the invalidation directly.

empathy_prompts: What might it feel like to have my own experience overridden?
Did I make the other person feel more clear and known, or more erased and unstable?
Would I trust someone who treated my experience this way?
love_repair: acknowledge that you invalidated their experience
invite them to restate what the event felt like from their side
separate your perspective from their lived experience
repair the confusion or erasure caused

refusal_to_be_corrected.title: Refusal to be corrected
refusal_to_be_corrected.definition: Resisting, dismissing, or hardening against correction in a way that blocks growth, accountability, and repair.

scriptures.principle: Loving correction is part of wisdom.
Heeding correction leads toward understanding.
Be quick to hear and slow to speak.

common_forms: refusing feedback outright
treating correction as attack
hardening immediately when challenged
insisting on self-justification instead of teachability
harm_impact: can make the other person feel impossible to reach
can discourage honest feedback
can keep sinful patterns entrenched
can make repair feel hopeless or one-sided

guilt: I may be resisting correction that I need.
My hardness may be protecting me from growth and accountability.
Teachability is part of love and repentance.
shame: If I was corrected, I am being condemned as a person.
Because correction hurts, I am justified in rejecting it.
If I resist enough, I can avoid the truth.
love_led_reframe: Love remains teachable.
Correction can be painful without being destructive.
Repair begins with willingness to hear what I do not want to hear.

empathy_prompts: What does it feel like to bring concern to someone who refuses correction?
Did my response invite honesty or punish it?
Would I feel hopeful trying to repair with someone in my posture?
love_repair: acknowledge the resistant posture directly
reopen the correction with humility
ask what was hardest for the other person to say
practice receiving feedback before responding

one_sided_accountability.title: One-sided accountability
one_sided_accountability.definition: Demanding, emphasizing, or tracking another person's faults while minimizing, delaying, or avoiding accountability for one's own patterns and impact.

scriptures.principle: Deal honestly with your own fault before focusing on another's.
Proud self-justification is not true righteousness.
Confession includes naming one's own sin honestly.

common_forms: holding the other person under a microscope while escaping the same scrutiny
tracking their failures while overlooking your own patterns
making accountability flow mostly one direction
equating being hurt with being automatically righteous
harm_impact: can make the relationship feel unequal and unsafe
can produce exhaustion and hopelessness
can distort truth by protecting one side from real examination
can block mutual humility and repair

guilt: I may be treating accountability as something mainly for the other person.
I may be using their failures to avoid my own exposure.
Love and truth require shared honesty, not one-sided scrutiny.
shame: If I admit my side too, then their harm no longer matters.
Because I was hurt, my own accountability is secondary.
If I stay focused on their wrong, I can avoid facing mine.
love_led_reframe: Mutual accountability is not moral equivalence; it is truthful humility.
I can name real harm done to me without using it to hide my own sin.
Repair grows where accountability is honest in both directions.

empathy_prompts: What might it feel like to be held highly accountable by someone who avoids the same standard?
Did my posture communicate shared humility or protected superiority?
Would I feel safe and hopeful in a relationship where accountability worked this way?
love_repair: confess where accountability has been one-sided
apply the same honesty to yourself that you expect from the other person
separate valid concern from protective asymmetry
rebuild mutual trust through balanced ownership

forced_reconciliation.title: Forced reconciliation
forced_reconciliation.definition: Pressuring, rushing, or demanding restored closeness, trust, peace, or emotional resolution before truth, safety, ownership, and repair have had time to take proper shape.

scriptures.principle: Pursue peace faithfully, but peace cannot be manufactured by pressure.
Humility, patience, and gentleness are necessary in preserving unity.
Love is patient.

common_forms: pressuring the harmed person to move on too quickly
demanding emotional closeness before trust is rebuilt
calling unresolved hurt unforgiveness too quickly
treating pace as rebellion rather than part of healing
seeking relational comfort before real repair
harm_impact: can make the harmed person feel spiritually or relationally pressured
can erase the time needed for safety and trust to rebuild
can make forgiveness language feel unsafe
can force appearance of peace without actual peace

guilt: I may be trying to reach reconciliation faster than truth and repair allow.
My desire for relief may be pressuring the other person's pace.
Love does not rush healing for its own comfort.
shame: If reconciliation is not immediate, there is no hope.
Because I want peace now, their slower pace is the real problem.
If I feel sorry, trust should return on my timetable.
love_led_reframe: Love is patient with the pace of rebuilding trust.
Reconciliation cannot be forced; it must be grown through truth and faithfulness.
Repair includes honoring the other person's process instead of trying to control it.

empathy_prompts: What might it feel like to be pushed toward closeness before safety has returned?
Did my urgency serve healing, or mainly my own relief?
Would I feel safe if reconciliation were demanded from me this way?
love_repair: acknowledge the pressure or rushing directly
honor the other person's pace without arguing it
focus on concrete repair rather than demanding emotional resolution
let trust rebuild through time and consistency

silent_scorekeeping.title: Silent scorekeeping
silent_scorekeeping.definition: Keeping an internal ledger of wrongs, disappointments, or perceived debts that quietly shapes tone, distance, reactions, and moral posture without being processed openly and honestly.

scriptures.principle: Love keeps no running record of wrongs.
Bitterness must be put away as forgiveness and tenderness grow.
Bitterness can grow and defile many.

common_forms: tracking past failures internally
quietly holding moral debt over the other person
treating present moments through accumulated grievance
acting from an internal ledger not openly named
harm_impact: can make the relationship feel mysteriously loaded
can create chronic suspicion and hidden punishment
can make present conversations feel unfairly weighted
can turn unresolved grievance into ongoing atmosphere

guilt: I may be carrying an internal record that is affecting how I speak and respond.
Unprocessed grievance may be shaping the relationship in hidden ways.
Love calls me to bring hurt into the light rather than let it harden silently.
shame: If I have been silently scorekeeping, all my pain must have been fake.
Because the wrongs were real, the hidden ledger is automatically righteous.
If I say it out loud, I lose all moral ground.
love_led_reframe: Love does not deny hurt, but it does refuse hidden bookkeeping as a way of relating.
I can bring accumulated pain into honest process rather than quiet control.
Repair may require naming what has been silently carried.

empathy_prompts: What might it feel like to be treated through a hidden ledger I cannot see?
Did my posture toward the other person feel transparent and fair, or quietly punishing?
Would I feel safe in a relationship shaped by unspoken bookkeeping?
love_repair: acknowledge the hidden ledger honestly
separate present issues from accumulated resentment
bring stored grievance into truthful processing
choose openness over hidden accounting

pride.title: Pride
pride.definition: An inflated self-protective posture that resists humility, correction, and the full truth about one's own impact.

scriptures.principle: Pride goes before destruction.
Humility counts others significant.
God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble.

common_forms: needing to be right above being loving
resisting correction
treating humility as weakness
protecting ego over relationship
harm_impact: can block repentance
can make others feel unsafe bringing concerns
can distort communication toward self-protection
can harden repeated sinful patterns

guilt: Pride may be shaping how I speak and respond.
I may be guarding ego more than love.
Humility is needed for repair.
shame: If pride is present, I am beyond hope.
Since humility feels painful, I should avoid it.
Exposure means only condemnation.
love_led_reframe: Godly conviction invites humility, not despair.
Love is willing to become low in order to repair.
The opposite of pride is not self-hatred but truthful humility.

empathy_prompts: How might my pride make the other person feel unseen or unsafe?
Did my need to be right make it harder to love well?
Would I feel emotionally safe raising concerns with someone in my posture?
love_repair: choose humility over self-protection
welcome correction without immediate counter-defense
admit wrong specifically
let love matter more than ego

self_protection.title: Self-protection above love
self_protection.definition: A heart pattern that prioritizes avoiding discomfort, exposure, consequence, or vulnerability over truth, empathy, and faithful repair.

scriptures.principle: Love must be in deed and truth.
Attend not only to your own interests.
Love for one another marks discipleship.

common_forms: hiding to avoid consequence
arguing to avoid ownership
withholding to avoid exposure
focusing on self-relief instead of the other's harm
harm_impact: can make the harmed person feel alone in carrying the cost
can block honesty and repair
can deepen mistrust
can train the relationship to revolve around the wrongdoer's comfort

guilt: I may be prioritizing my comfort over love.
My instinct to hide or defend may be increasing harm.
Love requires a willingness to bear discomfort for the sake of truth and repair.
shame: If I feel exposed, I must protect myself at all costs.
Discomfort means I am being destroyed rather than refined.
My pain cancels out my responsibility.
love_led_reframe: Love moves toward truth even when truth costs me.
Conviction calls me out of self-protection and into repair.
I can endure discomfort without making it the center.

empathy_prompts: What burden did my self-protection place on the other person?
Did my comfort become more important than their safety or clarity?
What might love have required from me in that moment?
love_repair: tell the truth without curating it
choose empathy before image-management
stay present to the other's pain
accept discomfort as part of repentance

hardness_of_heart.title: Hardness of heart
hardness_of_heart.definition: An inward resistance to tenderness, humility, correction, or compassion that makes a person less responsive to truth and to the pain of others.

scriptures.principle: God gives a new heart, replacing stone with living responsiveness.
Do not be hardened by sin's deceitfulness.
Be kind, tenderhearted, and forgiving.

common_forms: coldness toward another person's pain
refusal to soften under correction
chronic emotional distance
treating compassion as weakness
harm_impact: can make the other person feel unreachable and unimportant
can block repentance and repair
can turn communication into force rather than love
can keep conflict stuck in distance and defensiveness

guilt: My heart may be growing less tender toward truth and toward the other person.
Hardness may be shaping the way I respond.
I need softness, humility, and responsiveness before God.
shame: If hardness is present, I am permanently ruined.
Tenderness is unrealistic, so there is no point trying.
Distance is safer than love.
love_led_reframe: Godly conviction softens what sin hardens.
Love is not weakness; tenderness is part of strength.
Repair requires a heart willing to be moved.

empathy_prompts: How might my hardness feel to the person trying to reach me?
Did my posture communicate care or emotional stonewalling?
Would I feel safe bringing pain to someone in my posture?
love_repair: ask God for tenderness and humility
stay present to the other's pain without shutting down
confess coldness or emotional distance directly
practice responsiveness instead of resistance

fear_of_exposure.title: Fear of exposure
fear_of_exposure.definition: A heart pattern that reacts to being seen, corrected, or found out with concealment, defensiveness, distortion, or withdrawal rather than repentance.

scriptures.principle: Concealing wrong blocks mercy; confession opens the way.
Walking in the light includes honest confession and cleansing.
Hiding sin is heavy; confession brings relief.

common_forms: hiding details to avoid being known
defending quickly when confronted
retreating when truth gets close
reshaping the story to avoid exposure
harm_impact: can make truth fragmented and unstable
can shift burden onto the harmed person to keep uncovering reality
can block trust and repair
can keep relationship stuck in guessing and vigilance

guilt: Fear of being seen may be shaping my responses more than love.
My attempts to avoid exposure may be multiplying harm.
Truth in the light is necessary for repair.
shame: If I am fully seen, I will be destroyed.
Hiding is the only way to survive correction.
Exposure means only condemnation, never repair.
love_led_reframe: Conviction invites truth, not concealment.
Being seen honestly is part of healing, not only threat.
Love chooses the light even when it is costly.

empathy_prompts: What burden does my hiding place on the other person?
How might repeated concealment affect their sense of safety and trust?
Did I make them work to uncover what I should have brought into the light myself?
love_repair: tell the truth without waiting to be cornered
acknowledge how concealment affected safety
accept exposure as part of repentance
practice honesty sooner, not later

desire_for_control.title: Desire for control
desire_for_control.definition: A heart pattern that prioritizes controlling outcomes, perceptions, timing, or another person's response over trust, humility, consent, and love.

scriptures.principle: Worldly domination is not the way of Christ.
Do not domineer over others.
Humility looks to the good of others, not only one's own agenda.

common_forms: needing to manage the outcome at all costs
pressuring timing or responses
controlling the story or narrative
treating another person's freedom as a threat
harm_impact: can make the other person feel overruled or cornered
can damage trust and consent
can turn communication into a battle for control
can keep love secondary to outcome-management

guilt: I may be valuing control more than love.
My need to manage the situation may be harming safety and freedom.
I need to repent of forcing outcomes rather than trusting truth and love.
shame: If I let go of control, everything will fall apart.
Because I fear disorder, domination is justified.
If I am not in control, I am unsafe.
love_led_reframe: Love does not require domination.
I can pursue truth and repair without controlling another person's process.
Trusting God includes surrendering the urge to manage everyone else.

empathy_prompts: How might my control feel to the other person?
Did I leave room for their freedom and dignity?
Would I feel safe with someone who approached me this way?
love_repair: name the controlling posture honestly
release pressure and leverage
make room for the other person's pace and response
practice asking rather than forcing

resentment.title: Resentment
resentment.definition: A simmering inward posture of bitterness, scorekeeping, grievance-holding, or quiet hostility that shapes communication away from love and toward punishment, distance, or accusation.

scriptures.principle: Bitterness must be put away, and tenderhearted forgiveness pursued.
A root of bitterness can grow and defile many.
Love keeps no running record of wrongs.

common_forms: keeping score internally
using old pain to justify present harshness
coldness shaped by unresolved grievance
communicating from stored-up bitterness rather than present truth
harm_impact: can make current conversations feel loaded and unsafe
can turn correction into punishment
can intensify suspicion and negative interpretation
can erode tenderness and goodwill

guilt: Resentment may be shaping my tone and interpretations.
Stored bitterness may be making my communication less truthful and less loving.
I need to bring unresolved grievance into the light before it governs my words.
shame: Because resentment is present, I am only a bitter person with no hope.
If I have held onto pain this long, repair is pointless.
My stored anger automatically justifies how I speak.
love_led_reframe: Love does not build communication on hidden scorekeeping.
Conviction invites me to face bitterness rather than let it steer me.
Repair may include both confession and honest processing of unresolved hurt.

empathy_prompts: How might resentment make the other person feel in my presence?
Did my words reflect current reality, or stored-up grievance?
Would I feel safe if someone spoke to me from quiet bitterness?
love_repair: acknowledge the bitterness honestly
separate present concerns from stored grievance
process unresolved hurt without weaponizing it
choose honest truth over punishing tone

self_righteousness.title: Self-righteousness
self_righteousness.definition: A heart posture that magnifies another person's wrong while minimizing one's own need for humility, mercy, repentance, and accountability.

scriptures.principle: God opposes proud self-justification and receives humble repentance.
Deal honestly with the log in your own eye.
Do not think of yourself more highly than you ought.

common_forms: moral superiority in conflict
focusing on the other's sin while avoiding your own
using truth as proof of your greater righteousness
speaking as though correction only needs to go one direction
harm_impact: can make the other person feel judged rather than engaged
can block mutual humility and repair
can turn conflict into moral hierarchy instead of truth and love
can make confession and empathy harder

guilt: I may be approaching this from superiority rather than humility.
I may be emphasizing another person's wrong to avoid my own need for repentance.
Love corrects without exalting self.
shame: If self-righteousness is present, I must never speak about wrong again.
Because I see real sin, my own posture does not matter.
If I am more correct, humility becomes optional.
love_led_reframe: Humility and truth must stay together.
I can name wrong clearly without placing myself above the other person.
Love stays teachable even while confronting sin.

empathy_prompts: How might my posture feel to the other person?
Did my communication invite humility, or did it mainly project superiority?
Would I feel safe being corrected by someone in my posture?
love_repair: confess superiority or moral pride directly
name your own need for grace and correction
address wrong without elevating yourself
let humility shape both tone and content

indifference.title: Indifference / lack of love
indifference.definition: A heart posture of disengagement, apathy, or emotional distance in which another person's pain, dignity, or well-being is treated as unimportant or burdensome.

scriptures.principle: Look not only to your own interests, but also to the interests of others.
Love does not close its heart when another is in need.
Be devoted to one another in love and honor.

common_forms: not caring enough to engage
treating another person's distress as inconvenient
withdrawing concern emotionally
responding with cold passivity rather than attentive love
harm_impact: can make the other person feel invisible or unworthy of care
can intensify loneliness and discouragement
can make relationship feel one-sided or emotionally unsafe
can turn pain into abandonment

guilt: I may be acting as though the other person's pain does not matter very much.
Apathy may be shaping my lack of response.
Love requires more than avoiding dramatic harm; it requires engaged care.
shame: If indifference is present, I am permanently incapable of love.
Because I feel detached, care is no longer my responsibility.
If I do not feel warmth naturally, I am excused from acting in love.
love_led_reframe: Love can be chosen in action even when warmth feels weak.
Conviction invites me out of apathy and into attentive care.
Repair includes acknowledging the loneliness my indifference created.

empathy_prompts: What might it feel like to hurt near me and sense that I do not really care?
Did my posture communicate that the other person mattered?
Would I feel emotionally safe being vulnerable with someone in my posture?
love_repair: acknowledge the coldness or lack of response
move toward practical attentiveness
ask what care would help now
treat engagement as part of love, not an optional extra

envy.title: Envy
envy.definition: A resentful or restless inward response to another person's good, dignity, closeness, strength, blessings, or position, which can distort communication and make love harder.

scriptures.principle: Envy and selfish ambition lead to disorder and evil practice.
Love does not envy.
Envy rots the bones.

common_forms: resenting another person's strength or favor
feeling threatened by another person's goodness or position
quiet hostility toward what another person has
using words to diminish what is good in another person
harm_impact: can turn communication sour and comparative
can make generosity difficult
can distort how another person's actions are interpreted
can feed resentment, belittling, or rivalry

guilt: I may be reacting against another person's good rather than rejoicing in it.
Envy may be souring my words and interpretations.
Love does not need the other person to become smaller.
shame: If envy is present, I am only rotten inside.
Because I feel threatened, comparison is inevitable and harmless.
If I cannot rejoice naturally, I am excused from trying.
love_led_reframe: Love rejoices in another person's good.
Conviction invites me to release rivalry and choose gratitude.
I do not need the other person diminished in order to be safe.

empathy_prompts: How might envy change the way I speak to or about this person?
Did my words honor their dignity, or try to reduce it?
Would I feel safe around someone reacting to me from envy?
love_repair: confess envy honestly before God
stop using words to diminish the other person
practice gratitude and blessing rather than comparison
choose honor where rivalry was growing

fear_driven_control.title: Fear-driven control
fear_driven_control.definition: Using control, pressure, overmanagement, or domination as a way to protect oneself from fear, uncertainty, vulnerability, or loss.

scriptures.principle: Cast anxiety on God because He cares for you.
Bring anxiety to God rather than letting it rule you.
Love does not insist on its own way.

common_forms: tightening control when anxious
using pressure to reduce uncertainty
overmanaging the other person's process
treating vulnerability as something to dominate rather than endure
harm_impact: can make the other person feel managed rather than loved
can reduce freedom and honesty
can create fear-based dynamics instead of trust-based ones
can turn internal fear into external pressure

guilt: My fear may be driving me to control rather than trust.
I may be transferring my anxiety onto the other person through pressure.
Love cannot grow where fear is steering domination.
shame: If I feel afraid, control is my only option.
Because my fear is intense, its effects on others are secondary.
If I let go of control, I will lose everything.
love_led_reframe: Fear can be brought to God instead of imposed on others.
Love chooses trust and honesty over anxious domination.
Repair includes naming the fear without excusing the control.

empathy_prompts: How might my fear-driven control feel to the other person?
Did my anxiety become something they had to carry in the form of pressure?
Would I feel free and safe under this kind of management?
love_repair: acknowledge the controlling effect of fear
separate your fear from the other person's responsibility
reduce pressure and restore freedom
practice trust instead of overmanagement

people_pleasing_instead_of_truth.title: People-pleasing instead of truth
people_pleasing_instead_of_truth.definition: Shaping words, silence, agreement, or presentation primarily to avoid discomfort, disapproval, or conflict rather than to act in honest love.

scriptures.principle: Seeking human approval above faithfulness distorts service.
Truth must be spoken in love.
Fear of man is a snare.

common_forms: saying what seems easiest instead of what is true
avoiding needed truth to keep peace externally
false agreement to prevent discomfort
curating words mainly to avoid another person's reaction
harm_impact: can produce hidden dishonesty and confusion
can delay needed truth and repair
can make agreement feel unstable or performative
can burden the relationship with hidden reality

guilt: I may be prioritizing approval or peacekeeping over truth.
Avoided truth can still wound a relationship.
Love needs honesty, not only pleasantness.
shame: If I avoid truth, I should stop trying to speak altogether.
Because conflict scares me, dishonesty is harmless.
If everyone stays calm, the hidden reality does not matter.
love_led_reframe: Love tells the truth with gentleness, not with avoidance.
Peace without truth is fragile and costly.
Repair includes bringing hidden truth into the light carefully and honestly.

empathy_prompts: How might hidden truth affect the other person's trust if they later discover it?
Did my pleasantness serve love, or did it mainly protect me from discomfort?
Would I want agreement from someone who was not being fully truthful with me?
love_repair: acknowledge where approval-seeking replaced honesty
bring the withheld truth into the light gently
separate gentleness from avoidance
practice truthful love instead of performative peace

cold_superiority.title: Cold superiority
cold_superiority.definition: An inward posture of emotional distance, moral height, or detached judgment that treats another person as beneath warmth, tenderness, or mutual humility.

scriptures.principle: Humility counts others significant.
Honor one another above yourselves.
Self-exalting righteousness is not the posture God commends.

common_forms: emotionally cool moral height
detached judgment without tenderness
acting as though warmth is unnecessary because one is right
treating the other person as lesser or immature without humility
harm_impact: can make correction feel like being looked down on
can create emotional distance and hopelessness
can make the other person feel beneath care
can block mutuality and teachability

guilt: I may be relating from superiority instead of humble love.
My coolness may be communicating judgment without tenderness.
Love does not require me to become morally high and emotionally far away.
shame: If superiority is present, I must never say anything corrective again.
Because I see real wrong, tenderness is optional.
Distance proves maturity more than humility does.
love_led_reframe: Love remains low, near, and humane even while confronting sin.
Humility and warmth are not weakness; they are part of faithfulness.
Repair includes stepping down from emotional height and drawing nearer in love.

empathy_prompts: How might my cold superiority feel to the person in front of me?
Did my posture communicate honor, or did it mainly communicate distance and judgment?
Would I feel safe receiving correction from someone standing over me like this?
love_repair: confess the superior posture honestly
repair the emotional distance it created
re-approach with humility and warmth
let truth come through tenderness rather than detachment

self_pity_as_deflection.title: Self-pity as deflection
self_pity_as_deflection.definition: Turning quickly toward one's own pain, shame, burden, or emotional overwhelm in a way that redirects attention away from the harm done and softens accountability.

scriptures.principle: Look not only to your own interests, but also to the interests of others.
Godly grief produces repentance; self-absorbed sorrow does not.
Be quick to hear, especially when the other person is in pain.

common_forms: making your shame the center instead of the impact
moving quickly into your own emotional collapse
seeking comfort before ownership
using your distress to soften what the other person is saying
harm_impact: can make the harmed person caretake the wrongdoer emotionally
can push impact out of focus
can derail accountability and repair
can leave the other person feeling unseen and burdened

guilt: I may be centering my own pain in a way that moves focus away from the harm I caused.
My distress may be real, but it cannot replace ownership.
Love allows me to feel sorrow without making my sorrow the center.
shame: If I feel terrible, that itself should count as repair.
Because I am overwhelmed, I do not need to stay with the other person's pain.
My shame should now become the primary issue in the room.
love_led_reframe: Godly sorrow leads outward into repentance and repair.
I can hold my own pain without making the harmed person carry it for me.
Love keeps impact in view even when I feel exposed.

empathy_prompts: What might it feel like if the person who hurt me quickly made their own pain the center?
Did my response require the other person to comfort me instead of being cared for themselves?
Would I feel safe bringing pain to someone who responded this way?
love_repair: acknowledge where self-focused sorrow displaced ownership
return focus to the harm and impact
seek support for your distress without burdening the harmed person with it
let sorrow produce specific repentance and repair
